topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. A profile picture loaded: a grainy, slightly distorted photo of a face I hadn't seen in fifteen years, a face etched with a familiar, sneering confidence. My breath hitched. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of most of my awkward middle school years, the one who used to make my stomach clench just thinking about him. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, tracing the outline of the screen with a restless energy. I scrolled through his profile—empty, almost aggressively curated—and felt a cold knot tighten in my chest. Why now? After all this time, why reach out? I didn't click anything. I just put the phone face down on the desk, the silence of my room suddenly feeling heavy, charged with unspoken history.

[story 2]

When the chime sounded, I glanced at the caller ID. It was an old number, one I hadn't thought about in years, but the name displayed made my jaw tighten. It was him. The memory of his laughter, sharp and cruel, flashed behind my eyes, instantly replaced by a defensive tightening in my shoulders. I didn't answer immediately. I let the phone ring twice, then finally picked up, my voice deliberately flat. "Hello?" The voice on the other end was familiar, laced with a practiced nonchalance that felt entirely too smooth. I listened, my knuckles whitening around the device. Every word he spoke seemed carefully measured, a performance I couldn't quite decipher. A strange prickle ran down my arms, a sensation like static electricity building just beneath my skin. I ended the call without saying anything, tossing the phone onto the bed as if it were suddenly hot.

[story 3]

The incoming request materialized on my social feed. It was an invitation from someone from my past, someone whose presence always brought a distinct chill. I stared at the profile picture, noticing the subtle shift in his expression—less the aggressive smirk I remembered, more something unnervingly placid. A tremor started in my hands as I navigated the options. I didn't want to engage. My gaze flickered toward the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, the grey light mirroring the unsettled feeling inside me. I considered deleting the request, but a stubborn inertia kept my thumb hovering over the confirmation. I took a deep, slow breath, trying to steady the erratic rhythm of my heart. This felt like walking toward an unknown threshold, and the ground beneath my feet felt unsteady.
